moving-around.label: Moving Around with cd
moving-around.summary: Use cd, .., ., and ~ to move between folders safely.

try_moving_around_sketch0.title: Build a cd practice path
try_moving_around_sketch0.prompt: Create `cd-practice/level1/level2/checkpoint.txt`. Practice using `cd` to move through the folders.

try_moving_around_sketch1.title: Practice moving up one folder
try_moving_around_sketch1.prompt: Create `up-one-lab/inside/done.txt`. Use `cd ..` at least once while practicing.

try_moving_around_sketch2.title: Create a path trail
try_moving_around_sketch2.prompt: Create `trail/start/middle/end.txt` to show you can work with nested folders.

sc-cd.title: Which command moves into a folder named notes?
sc-cd.prompt: Use cd followed by the folder name.
sc-cd.hint: Use cd followed by the folder name.

help.concept: Use cd followed by the folder name.
help.hint_1: Use cd followed by the folder name.
help.hint_2: Review the command names from the lesson, then choose the option that matches the task.

sc-parent.title: What does cd .. usually do?
sc-parent.prompt: Two dots refer to the parent folder.
sc-parent.hint: Two dots refer to the parent folder.

help.concept: Two dots refer to the parent folder.
help.hint_1: Two dots refer to the parent folder.
help.hint_2: Review the command names from the lesson, then choose the option that matches the task.

sc-home.title: What does ~ represent in Linux paths?
sc-home.prompt: It is a shortcut to a user's home folder.
sc-home.hint: It is a shortcut to a user's home folder.

help.concept: It is a shortcut to a user's home folder.
help.hint_1: It is a shortcut to a user's home folder.
help.hint_2: Review the command names from the lesson, then choose the option that matches the task.

mc-navigation-habits.title: Choose good navigation habits.
mc-navigation-habits.prompt: Think about checking your location and nearby files.
mc-navigation-habits.hint: Think about checking your location and nearby files.

help.concept: Think about checking your location and nearby files.
help.hint_1: Think about checking your location and nearby files.
help.hint_2: Review the command names from the lesson, then choose the option that matches the task.

options.a: Run pwd if you feel lost
options.b: Run ls before changing important files
options.c: Use cd folder-name to enter folders
options.d: Use rm whenever you want to move

ci-build-cd-practice.title: Build a cd practice path
ci-build-cd-practice.prompt:
Create `cd-practice/level1/level2/checkpoint.txt`. Practice using `cd` to move through the folders.

Use the terminal. The editor is hidden on purpose for this task.
ci-build-cd-practice.hint: Use terminal commands such as `pwd`, `ls`, `mkdir -p`, `touch`, `cp`, `mv`, or `rm` depending on the task.

help.concept: This is a Linux terminal workspace task. Complete it by changing the file tree with terminal commands.
help.hint_1: Use `pwd` and `ls` to inspect where you are.
help.hint_2: The checker looks for the required folders/files and checks that forbidden files are gone.

ci-build-cd-practice.placeholder: Use the terminal to complete this task.

cd-enters-folders.title: cd changes the current folder
cd-enters-folders.bodyMarkdown:
`cd` means **change directory**.

```bash
cd folder-name
```

After using `cd`, run `pwd` to confirm where you are. Then run `ls` to see what is inside that folder.

dot-dot-parent.title: .. means the parent folder
dot-dot-parent.bodyMarkdown:
`..` means “go up one folder.”

```bash
cd ..
```

`.` means “this folder.” You will mostly use `..` when moving back out of a folder after entering it.

home-shortcut.title: ~ points to your home folder
home-shortcut.bodyMarkdown:
The shortcut `~` means your home folder.

```bash
cd ~
```

Inside this course workspace, most tasks should stay in the provided workspace. Use `pwd` if you ever feel lost.
